zh:
日本神话的骨架来自八世纪的两部官修史书：《古事记》（712年）与《日本书纪》（720年），合称「记纪」。神代卷从天地初开讲起：伊邪那岐与伊邪那美搅动沧海造出列岛，三贵子分掌太阳、月亮与海原，高天原的天津神与出云的国津神彼此角力，最终以天孙降临收束——天皇的世系由此接上众神的谱系。各地《风土记》与《万叶集》则保存了正史之外的地方异传。

与神话并行的是妖怪的传统。天狗、河童、鬼、妖狐从中世的说话集与绘卷里走出：《今昔物语集》记天狗惑僧，《大江山绘词》画鬼王授首，御伽草子把玉藻前的故事讲进宫廷与荒野。江户时代，鸟山石燕的《画图百鬼夜行》把口耳相传的精怪整理成图鉴式的谱系；明治以后，柳田国男的《远野物语》又把东北山村的河童与座敷童子原样记录下来。妖怪住在人的世界的边缘——山口、水边、家宅的座敷，与人立约、相扑、恶作剧，也随人一起迁居。

这个体系没有全知全能的主神：八百万神各居其所，神与妖之间的界限流动不定，一柄剑、一块石头、一座旧宅都可能有灵。神佛习合一千余年，佛教的堕落僧、大陆的星宿之名与列岛自己的山川精灵在同一张谱系里杂居。伊势二十年一度的式年迁宫、高千穗的夜神乐、石见神乐里喷烟的大蛇，至今仍在把这些故事一遍遍演给活人看。
en:
The skeleton of Japanese mythology comes from two court-commissioned histories of the eighth century, the Kojiki (712) and the Nihon Shoki (720), known together as the Kiki. Their opening books run from the parting of heaven and earth: Izanagi and Izanami churn the sea into islands, the three noble children take the sun, the moon, and the sea, the heavenly gods of Takamagahara contend with the earthly gods of Izumo, and the cycle closes with the heavenly grandson descending to rule, splicing the imperial line onto the family tree of the gods. The provincial gazetteers and the Man'yoshu anthology preserve local variants the official books left out.

Alongside the myths runs the yokai tradition. Tengu, kappa, oni, and fox spirits step out of medieval tale collections and picture scrolls: the Konjaku Monogatarishu has tengu beguiling monks, the Oeyama scroll shows the demon king losing his head, and Muromachi storybooks carry Tamamo no Mae from the palace to the moors. In the Edo period Toriyama Sekien organized the creatures of hearsay into illustrated catalogues, and in 1910 Yanagita Kunio's Tono Monogatari wrote down the kappa and house spirits of northern villages as the villagers told them. Yokai live at the edges of the human world, at mountain passes, riversides, and parlors of old houses, making bargains, wrestling, playing pranks, and sometimes moving house along with the family.

The system has no omniscient overlord. The eight million gods each keep their own seat, the line between god and monster stays porous, and a sword, a stone, or an old room may hold a spirit. A millennium of Shinto-Buddhist fusion left fallen monks, continental star-omens, and the archipelago's own mountain and river spirits sharing one genealogy. The twenty-year rebuilding of Ise, the night kagura of Takachiho, and the smoke-breathing serpent of Iwami kagura still perform these stories for the living.
